The bug was introduced because we reused the badge visibility logic from the Charity site but referenced environment configuration from the Buyer site.

For the Carrier site, we implemented the same logic used on the Charity site to show/hide the badge. On the Charity site, the “NEXT_PUBLIC_ENVIRONMENT” variable is not defined in its .env file. Because of that, the logic treated this variable as undefined and hid the badge, which was the expected behaviour there.
However, when setting up the Carrier site, we referred to the Buyer site’s .env configuration. In the Buyer site, NEXT_PUBLIC_ENVIRONMENT is defined with the value "Production".
As a result, on the Carrier site, NEXT_PUBLIC_ENVIRONMENT was also defined, so the logic did not treat it as undefined and did not hide the badge, causing the bug.
I stil see multiple places where “production”, “prod” etc…used. What is that for?
, do we have any common function in UX framework which we can use in all frontends that derives badge display name?

Here, “NODE_ENV” is a standard Node.js environment variable that Next.js automatically sets to 'production' when the app is built and started (next build / next start), and 'development' when running locally (next dev). The references to “production” in the code (e.g., NODE_ENV === 'production') are used to distinguish between local development and a deployed production build, not between our dev/staging/prod environments. These checks are intentional and handle differences between local and deployed execution—such as HTTPS setup, routing behavior behind the gateway, CDN usage, and secure cookie handling.
For “prod”, it is the value of ENVIRONMENT_NAME set in
,   which we are using now for the badge hide/show logic.
